title: The thermopower properties of interacting systems
authors: M. A. Habitzreuter, Willdauany C. de Freitas da Silva, Rodrigo A. Fontenele, Natanael C. Costa, Thereza Paiva
abstract: The quest for efficient devices has fueled research in thermoelectric materials. In these materials, the goal is to maximize the Figure of Merit $ZT$. One of the components of this quantity is the Seebeck coefficient, which measures the voltage generated in response to a temperature gradient. Recent studies have revealed that strong electronic correlations can enhance the Seebeck coefficient, leading to anomalous behavior near half-filling. However, the impact of interactions beyond the on-site Hubbard remains mostly unexplored. In this work, we investigate the Seebeck coefficient considering attractive interactions, nearest-neighbor interactions, sublattice potentials and electron-phonon coupling. We find that additional interaction scales can enhance the Seebeck coefficient, while also leading to multiple anomalous changes of sign as a function of doping. We also show that the anomalous behavior is connected to a gap opening in the ground state. Moreover, electron-phonon coupling also lead to a Seebeck anomaly, even without on-site repulsion. We connect these changes of sign in the Seebeck coefficient with a restructuring of the Fermi surface and a change in its topology, an effect commonly seen in cuprates.
summary: This paper explores how various types of electronic interactions and electron-phonon coupling influence the Seebeck coefficient in 2D systems. It demonstrates that these interactions can trigger anomalous sign changes in thermopower by driving phase transitions and restructuring the Fermi surface. The findings provide a deeper understanding of the thermoelectric properties of complex materials like cuprates and transition-metal dichalcogenides.
main_problem: Investigating how electronic interactions beyond the on-site Hubbard model, such as nearest-neighbor interactions, sublattice potentials, and electron-phonon coupling, affect the thermopower (Seebeck coefficient) and lead to anomalous behavior.
main_result: Additional interaction scales can enhance the Seebeck coefficient and cause multiple anomalous sign changes linked to gap opening and Fermi surface reconstruction. Electron-phonon coupling can also induce Seebeck anomalies even without on-site repulsion.
method: The study uses Determinant Quantum Monte Carlo (DQMC) for thermodynamic and transport properties, supplemented by Exact Diagonalization (ED) and Cluster Perturbation Theory (CPT).
model_or_system: The research focuses on 2D lattice models including the Hubbard, Extended Hubbard, Holstein-Hubbard, and Ionic Hubbard models, incorporating kinetic energy, on-site (U) and nearest-neighbor (V) interactions, and electron-phonon coupling (g).
key_observables: Seebeck coefficient (Kelvin formula), entropy, electronic density, charge structure factor, and spectral functions.
important_parameters: On-site interaction (U), nearest-scale interaction (V), electron-phonon coupling (g), staggered potential (delta), temperature (T), and electron density (n).
main_assumptions: The Trotter decomposition introduces a small discretization error of O(delta-tau^2), which is mitigated by keeping delta-tau <= 0.1.
figures_summary: Figures show density and entropy as functions of chemical potential, Seebeck coefficient anomalies across different interaction regimes, phase boundaries for CDW and SDW orders, and spectral functions illustrating Fermi surface reconstruction.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the importance of thermopower in correlated systems, details the various Hubbard-like models and DQMC methodology, presents results for different interaction scales (U, V, g, delta), connects these to gap opening and Fermi surface topology, and concludes with implications for materials like cuprates.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Color online) (a) Density n as a function of chemical potential µ, (b) Entropy, and (c) the Seebeck coefficient as a function of n for non-interacting, attractive, and repulsive interaction |U/t| = 6 and temperature T/t = 0.5.

FIG. 3. (Color online) (a) Seebeck coefficient as a function of density for the attractive case, with V/t = 0 and V/t = −1 (b) Repulsive case, with V/t = 0, 1, 2. Results are for temperature T/t = 0.5, with 10 × 10 and 8 × 8 lattices.

FIG. 2. (Color online) (a), (c) Density n as a function of chemical potential µ, (b), (d) Entropy as a function of den- sity n for attractive and repulsive interaction |U/t| = 6 and temperature T/t = 0.5.

FIG. 5. (Color online) Ground state CDW structure factor calculated with ED on a 4 × 4 lattice at quarter-filling. The black curve is the phase boundary calculated with CPT. Red stars indicate the parameters of our DQMC simulations for the Seebeck coefficient at quarter-filling.

FIG. 4. (Color online) Seebeck coefficient as a function of density for different temperatures T/t = 1, 0.67, 0.5 for inter- action strengths U/t = 6 and V/t = 2. The lowest temper- ature was obtained in a simulation on an 8 × 8 lattice due to the sign problem. The larger temperatures were simulated with a 10 × 10 lattice.
